questions.question_text: Which of the following is authorized to conduct a real estate closing in Nebraska?
questions.explanation: Nebraska is not an attorney-only closing state. Under Neb. Rev. Stat. §§ 76-2,121 and 76-2,122, a real estate closing agent must be licensed or regulated by a regulating entity (or employed by one). That covers NREC-licensed brokers, Nebraska attorneys, banks, savings institutions and credit unions, Nebraska trust companies, and title insurers and agents authorized by the Department of Insurance — but not appraisers, whose board is not a listed regulating entity.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants an out-of-state, FDIC-regulated bank to collect and disburse the funds at her Nebraska closing. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, FDIC-regulated banks may act as closing agents
distractors: No, only banks chartered in Nebraska may conduct closings
Only if a Nebraska attorney supervises the closing
No, banks may never serve as closing agents
questions.explanation: Under Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-2,122, anyone who collects and disburses funds to close a Nebraska real estate transaction must be licensed or regulated by a regulating entity, or employed by a regulated person or entity. The regulating entities listed in § 76-2,121 include the Nebraska Real Estate Commission, the Nebraska Supreme Court (attorneys), the Department of Insurance, the Nebraska Department of Banking and Finance, and federal regulators such as the FDIC, OCC, and NCUA. A bank regulated by the FDIC therefore qualifies even if it is not chartered in Nebraska. The closing agent must also disburse only good funds from a trust account at a federally insured institution.

questions.question_text: A Nebraska trust company offers to handle the closing for a commercial transaction. Is the trust company authorized?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, NE trust companies may conduct closings
distractors: No, only attorneys handle commercial closings
Only if the amount is under $500,000
Only if both parties consent in writing
questions.explanation: Yes. Nebraska trust companies are regulated by the Nebraska Department of Banking and Finance, one of the regulating entities listed in Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-2,121, so they may act as real estate closing agents under § 76-2,122. There is no dollar cap and no requirement that the parties consent in writing.

questions.question_text: A title insurer from another state wants to conduct closings in Nebraska. What must it have?
questions.correct_answer: Authorization to do business in Nebraska
questions.explanation: Title insurers must be authorized to do business in Nebraska before they can conduct real estate closings. Out-of-state title companies cannot simply begin closing transactions without meeting Nebraska's authorization requirements.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks whether Nebraska requires an attorney to be present at closing. What is the correct answer?
questions.correct_answer: No, Nebraska is not an attorney-only state
distractors: Yes, an attorney must supervise all closings
questions.explanation: Nebraska does not require attorney presence at closing. Multiple entity types are authorized to conduct closings, including real estate brokers, banks, credit unions, trust companies, and title insurers authorized in the state.

questions.question_text: A non-lawyer abstractor prepares an abstract of title. Who must render the title opinion based on that abstract?
questions.explanation: In Nebraska, non-lawyer abstractors may prepare abstracts of title, but only licensed attorneys may render title opinions. Interpreting the legal significance of the chain of title constitutes the practice of law.

questions.question_text: A Nebraska title insurance commitment is issued on March 1. Under the standard commitment terms used in Nebraska, by about what date must the closing occur before the commitment expires?
questions.explanation: Title commitments used in Nebraska (the standard ALTA form) are contractually valid for 180 days from the commitment date — a matter of standard practice, not a Nebraska statutory deadline. A commitment issued March 1 therefore expires around August 28; the closing must occur by then or a new or updated commitment must be obtained.

questions.question_text: Historically, which method of title assurance was most commonly used in Nebraska before title insurance became dominant?
questions.explanation: Nebraska historically relied on abstracts of title reviewed by attorneys to establish title assurance. While title insurance has now become the predominant method, the abstract-and-attorney-opinion approach remains a recognized practice.

questions.question_text: A buyer's lender requires title insurance, but the seller offers only an abstract with attorney opinion. What should the buyer do?
questions.correct_answer: Obtain title insurance for the lender
distractors: Accept the existing abstract as sufficient
Ask the NREC to mediate the dispute
Cancel the transaction and start over
questions.explanation: While abstracts with attorney opinions are still valid in Nebraska, most modern lenders require title insurance. The buyer must obtain title insurance to meet lender requirements, even though Nebraska recognizes both methods of title assurance.

questions.question_text: Who is permitted to prepare an abstract of title in Nebraska?
distractors: Only the county register of deeds
questions.explanation: Nebraska allows non-lawyers to prepare abstracts of title: under the Abstracters Act of 1985 (Neb. Rev. Stat. §§ 76-535 to 76-564), abstracters must be registered with the Abstracters Board of Examiners, but they need not be attorneys. Only attorneys, however, may render title opinions based on an abstract — interpreting the legal significance of the chain of title is the practice of law.

questions.question_text: A property sells for $250,000 in Nebraska in August 2026. What is the documentary stamp tax owed?
questions.explanation: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-901 sets the documentary stamp tax at $3.32 per $1,000 of value (or fraction thereof) for transfers before January 1, 2032, after which it is scheduled to drop to $2.32 (LB 1067 (2026), effective July 18, 2026). For a $250,000 sale: 250 × $3.32 = $830.00. Earlier rates: $2.25 per $1,000 through September 2, 2025, then $2.32 per $1,000 (LB 78 (2025)) until July 18, 2026.

questions.question_text: A deed is transferred between spouses as part of a divorce decree. Is documentary stamp tax required?
distractors: Yes, but at half the standard rate
Only if ordered by the court
questions.explanation: Nebraska exempts certain transfers from the documentary stamp tax, including transfers between spouses pursuant to a divorce decree, government transfers, and certain trust transfers.

questions.question_text: Buyer A records a deed on Monday. Buyer B purchased the same property and records Tuesday without knowledge of Buyer A. Who prevails?
distractors: Buyer B, as a bona fide purchaser
questions.explanation: Nebraska's recording act, Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-238, is race-notice: a later purchaser defeats an earlier conveyance only by both taking without notice and recording first. Buyer A recorded on Monday, so Buyer B cannot win the race to record, and A's recorded deed gives constructive notice to anyone acquiring an interest afterward. Buyer A prevails.

questions.question_text: Nebraska uses which type of recording system for real property conveyances?
questions.explanation: Under Neb. Rev. Stat. § 76-238(1), an unrecorded instrument is void as to creditors and subsequent purchasers in good faith without notice whose own instruments are recorded first. Because a subsequent purchaser must both take without notice AND win the race to record, Nebraska is a race-notice state (Kimco Addition v. Lower Platte South N.R.D., 232 Neb. 289 (1989); Miller v. McMillen, 214 Neb. 244 (1983)).

questions.question_text: The city of Omaha transfers a park to the county. Is documentary stamp tax owed on this conveyance?
distractors: Yes, but the county pays it
questions.explanation: Government transfers are exempt from the Nebraska documentary stamp tax. Other exemptions include divorce transfers and certain trust transfers. The exemption applies regardless of the property's value.
